arxiv_papers.title: Poverty traps are rare, but trappedness isn't
arxiv_papers.snippet: The persistence of poverty is not well explained by who is poor. We argue the relevant object of measurement is trappedness--expected escape time from deprivation--which varies systematically across institutional environments and is invisible to standard poverty indices. Using Markov chains estimate

arxiv_papers.title: Measuring the depth of multidimensional poverty with ordinal data
arxiv_papers.snippet: This paper proposes a positional poverty gap measure of multidimensional poverty within the Alkire-Foster counting framework. The measure captures the depth of deprivations even when indicators are ordinal, unlike the standard poverty gap, which requires cardinal variables. The proposed method draws

youtube_items.title: [YT] Wealth Inequality in America Has Never Been Worse. The Middle Class Has Disappeared
youtube_items.summary: Most people have no clue about how severe wealth inequality has become in America and how much worse it's getting. There's a YouTube video by user Paul Zane where he references a study done by a Harvard economist and professor and the study asked 5,000 Americans how they believed wealth was distributed in the United States. The group was first asked what they thought the wealth distribution in America looked like. Finding up American wealth into five segments, the participants came up with the following, a bar chart that looks like this. With the top 20% owning a little bit more than half of the nation's wealth and the bottom 20% owning just a sliver of wealth no more than 5%. Participants were then asked what they thought the ideal distribution should be. Over 9 out of 10 participants sai

youtube_items.title: [YT] Is inequality inevitable?
youtube_items.summary: In South Africa, one of the most unequal countries in the world, the richest one-tenth of one percent owns almost 30 percent of all the country's wealth. More than double what the bottom 90 percent owns. Income and wealth inequality are not new. In fact, economists and historians who've charted economic inequality throughout history haven't found a single society without it, which raises a bleak question. Is inequality inevitable? One way to estimate inequality is with a number called the genie index, which is calculated by comparing the income or wealth distribution of a perfectly equal society to the actual income or wealth distribution. The area of this shape multiplied by two is the genie index. The genie of one indicates perfect inequality. One person has everything and everyone else

youtube_items.title: [YT] How Hong Kong became one of the world’s most unequal cities
youtube_items.summary: It's a city where some people live in the lap of luxury, while others call metal cages home. Hong Kong is a paradise for those rich, and it's a hell for those poor. They say it is. Hong Kong's richest earn 81 times more than its poorest residents, according to median figures released in a report by the charity Augs Fem. That's $130,000, or roughly $16,562 US dollars per month, for people on the wealthiest end of the scale, but a mere $1600 or $203 US dollars for the poorest. And the problem is worsening. Hong Kong's wealth gap had more than doubled by the first quarter of 2024 compared to data from five years earlier.
